access.format: Restricted digital book / searchable bibliographic preview
notes: No unrestricted complete public scan of the 1957 edition was verified; restricted copies were not cached. Print copies are widely held and purchasable second-hand, but prices vary.

bibliographic.title: The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer

entities.description: The immediate printed source for the electronic text. The Canterbury Tales occupies pp. 17–265 in the source statement; Robinson uses the Chaucer Society's printed Ellesmere transcript as the base representation for an eclectic text and records selected variants.
entities.label: Robinson's 1957 second edition of The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer

rights.basis: The second edition was first published in 1957; no public-domain determination, rights-holder permission, or open license was verified.
rights.component: Robinson's 1957 editorial selection, emendations, notes, and apparatus
rights.jurisdiction: United States

access.accessMethod: U-M Corpus of Middle English browse records and searchable full-text transcriptions
access.entityLabel: Chaucer Society parallel-text publications of The Canterbury Tales
notes: These are searchable transcriptions of the eight printed Chaucer Society witness texts relevant to Robinson's described source set, not page-image facsimiles of the printed volumes or manuscripts.

rights.basis: The represented printed transcript publications date from 1868 through 1902, and U-M presents the digitized texts with a No Copyright–United States statement.
rights.component: Nineteenth-century printed parallel-text edition and editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: The locally cached Internet Archive PDF covers only Parts I–II; this public-domain assessment concerns the represented nineteenth- and early-twentieth-century printed layer, not modern provider metadata or terms.

access.accessMethod: Internet Archive item with a directly downloadable scan of Parts I–II
access.entityLabel: Chaucer Society parallel-text publications of The Canterbury Tales

localCopies.label: A Six-Text Print, Parts I–II
notes: The cached scan contains Parts I–II only; it is not a complete copy of the six-text series or the later Harley 7334 and Dd.4.24 transcript publications represented by this entity.
The provider PDF is readable as 346 pages, but qpdf reports malformed indirect references and linearization hint-table warnings; the cache preserves the provider bytes unchanged.

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A Six-Text Print of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales and separately issued Chaucer Society texts of Harley 7334 and Cambridge Dd.4.24, Chaucer Society, First Series (London, 1868–1902).
bibliographic.title: A Six-Text Print of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales

entities.description: Printed diplomatic witness texts begun in 1868 with Ellesmere, Hengwrt, Cambridge Gg.4.27, Corpus Christi College Oxford 198, Petworth, and Lansdowne 851, followed by the separately printed Harley 7334 text in 1885 and Cambridge Dd.4.24 text in 1902. Robinson's documented base and collation set uses these printed witness representations among its sources.
entities.label: Chaucer Society parallel-text publications of The Canterbury Tales
notes: This entity represents the printed parallel-text source layer rather than a claim that Robinson consulted every named physical manuscript directly.

rights.basis: The represented printed transcript publications date from 1868 through 1902, and U-M presents the digitized texts with a No Copyright–United States statement.
rights.component: Nineteenth-century printed parallel-text edition and editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: The locally cached Internet Archive PDF covers only Parts I–II; this public-domain assessment concerns the represented nineteenth- and early-twentieth-century printed layer, not modern provider metadata or terms.

access.accessMethod: Huntington restricts circulation of the manuscript and directs users to digital reproductions or the print facsimile
access.entityLabel: Huntington Library MS EL 26 C 9 (Ellesmere Chaucer)
access.provider: The Huntington Library, Art Museum, and Botanical Gardens

entities.description: A deluxe illuminated manuscript of The Canterbury Tales produced in England, cataloged c. 1400–1410. It underlies the Chaucer Society Ellesmere transcript used as Robinson's principal base representation and supplies the Ellesmere-related order, subject to Robinson's collation and emendation.

holding.notes: The physical manuscript is restricted; Huntington directs researchers to digital reproductions or the print facsimile.

entities.label: Huntington Library MS EL 26 C 9 (Ellesmere Chaucer)

physicalDescription.extent: 232 main text folios plus twelve cataloged non-text leaves (ii modern + iv contemporary before the text; iv modern + ii contemporary after it); 480 canvases in the current published IIIF sequence
notes: The official item record dates the manuscript c. 1400–1410; a separate institutional feature page uses the narrower c. 1400–1405 range.

rights.basis: The manuscript was produced c. 1400–1410.
rights.component: Medieval manuscript content as a historical object
rights.jurisdiction: United States

access.accessMethod: Official Huntington digital object and IIIF Presentation manifest
access.entityLabel: Huntington 480-canvas published IIIF presentation of the Ellesmere Chaucer

localCopies.label: Huntington Ellesmere Chaucer complete 480-canvas published object
notes: Complete official published IIIF sequence retrieved at the effective 1600-pixel width profile; complete coverage refers to the 480-canvas provider inventory, not every leaf in the physical extent statement.
ZIP contains the provider manifest, an exact-source inventory, and all 480 canvas images.

workPortion.label: The Canterbury Tales in the Ellesmere manuscript
notes: The work occupies all 232 main text folios. The complete published-object cache also retains eight preliminary and final leaves, while four additional non-text leaves listed in the physical extent are absent from the provider's published sequence.
A public viewer is not itself an affirmative image-reuse license; consult the separate rights record.

entities.description: The complete official 480-canvas published sequence, including all 232 main folios and eight published preliminary/final leaves. Its own physical description lists twelve non-text leaves, so the sequence is complete as a provider-published inventory but does not reproduce four cataloged non-text leaves. The manifest describes 800-DPI scans made from color transparencies photographed after the manuscript's 1995 conservation.

access.accessMethod: Apply to consult the original; the catalog says authorization is required and surrogates should normally be used
access.entityLabel: National Library of Wales Peniarth MS 392D (Hengwrt Chaucer)

entities.description: An early, incomplete and differently ordered manuscript of The Canterbury Tales, cataloged [1395]–[1405] and written mainly by the same scribe as Ellesmere. Scholarship cited here describes it as generally accepted as the earliest attempt to assemble the Tales, a qualified relative-priority claim despite overlapping catalog date ranges. It is an important comparator represented in the Chaucer Society parallel texts, not Robinson's base manuscript.

holding.notes: Original physical access is authorized only and surrogates should normally be used.

entities.label: National Library of Wales Peniarth MS 392D (Hengwrt Chaucer)

physicalDescription.extent: 258 folios total: four modern binding leaves, original parchment ff. 2–250, and four modern binding leaves
notes: The current red morocco binding dates from 1956; earlier covers are retained and included in the digital sequence.
The catalog records losses including Canon's Yeoman G.554–1481 and Parson/Retraction from X.1180 to the end.

rights.basis: The manuscript was produced c. 1395–1405.
rights.component: Medieval manuscript content as a historical object
rights.jurisdiction: United States

next_steps: Inspect upstream history outside the current one-commit CME import, if available, to identify “pfs” and the A.3931 editor.
Ask the CME maintainers for the December 2020 repair log and the source or collation authority for both readings.
Keep the exact diff, comment dates, and current U-M agreement documented without treating agreement as proof of the repair workflow.
openQuestions.question: Who is “pfs,” who made the unattributed A.3931 repair, and what textual authority supports the two December 2020 readings?

next_steps: Ask the CME maintainers to identify the intended license for source/recent_changes/repairs_2023/CT.xml and the repaired readings.
Do not infer that either the software-worded Unlicense or the CME_from_OTA folder notice controls the repaired path until scope is clarified.
Retain the exact path notice, repository license, XML headers, and version diff as separate evidence.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME_from_OTA path-specific CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 assertion extend to the closely related repaired CT XML outside that folder, and how does it interact with the repository Unlicense and embedded OTA notice?

next_steps: Request a written interpretation from U-M before redistributing the XML or a close derivative, especially in a commercial workflow.
Keep underlying-text copyright, editorial copyright, encoding rights, and provider terms distinct.
openQuestions.question: How do U-M's current No Copyright–United States statement, the embedded 1993 OTA personal-scholarly-use notice, and CME_from_OTA's CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 assertion interact for reuse of this encoding?

next_steps: Consult Robinson's full preface and textual notes in a lawful copy of the 1957 edition.
Model additional witnesses only when reading-level or section-level source roles can be demonstrated.
Do not replace the current focused path with a flat list that implies direct physical consultation.
openQuestions.question: For each reading in Robinson's Canterbury Tales, which evidence came from a directly consulted manuscript, a Chaucer Society printed transcript, Thynne, or another intermediary?

next_steps: Ask reference@huntington.org whether the IIIF JPEGs may be redistributed in this repository or commercial print products and request a citable written answer.
Until clarified, link and cache for research but do not treat the missing manifest license as an open license.
openQuestions.question: What item-specific reuse terms, if any, govern the Huntington Ellesmere IIIF images?

next_steps: Display Robinson's Ellesmere-related sequence as editorial.
Preserve fragment identifiers and ordering uncertainty in downstream interfaces.
openQuestions.question: What final order, if any, did Chaucer intend for the ten fragments?

reviewNotes: The abstract medieval work and its fifty-four represented component units are cataloged in the linked work-metadata manifest; lineage entities here are physical, editorial, or digital transmission objects.

The repaired publication XML and the CME_from_OTA copy entered the current CME Git history together. Their two running-text differences and December 2020 in-file correction comments are documented, but the shared import does not establish the complete repair workflow or authority for either reading.

Robinson's relationship to Ellesmere is routed through the Chaucer Society printed transcript because the checked evidence documents the printed source but does not prove direct autopsy of the Huntington codex. His edition remains eclectic rather than a diplomatic transcription.
Hengwrt is a major early comparator and a source in the Chaucer Society parallel-text layer; it is not claimed to be the immediate source of Robinson's whole text.

The CME_from_OTA path-specific CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 notice, current U-M No Copyright–United States language, the embedded 1993 Oxford Text Archive personal-scholarly-use notice, and the repository's software-worded Unlicense are recorded separately because their scopes and legal or contractual interaction are unresolved.

lineage.summary: The approved General Corpus publication source is the repaired repository XML at CME/source/recent_changes/repairs_2023/CT.xml. It is a corrected version of the repository's CME_from_OTA copy, which derives ultimately from the 1993 Oxford Text Archive/University of Michigan electronic text. That encoding represents The Canterbury Tales on pp. 17–265 of F. N. Robinson's 1957 second edition of The Works of Geoffrey Chaucer while omitting the edition's front and back matter and notes. Robinson's eclectic text uses the Chaucer Society's printed Ellesmere transcript as its principal base representation and collates other printed Chaucer Society transcripts, among additional sources. Ellesmere and Hengwrt are distinct manuscript witnesses; their complete provider-published IIIF inventories are cached locally with exact work portions recorded, while physical-object completeness is stated separately.

catalogSummary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
catalogSummary.date: Begun in the late 1380s; worked on into the 1390s, unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
catalogSummary.region: England; associated with late-medieval London literary language; exact place of composition uncertain
catalogSummary.title: The Canterbury Tales

catalogingSubject.label: The Canterbury Tales
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's unfinished Middle English pilgrimage frame narrative and tale collection, including its General Prologue, twenty-four pilgrim tales, linking passages, prologues, epilogues, and Retraction. It is distinct from any one manuscript witness, Robinson's 1957 eclectic scholarly edition, the 1993 OTA/U-M encoding, and either repository XML copy. The ten-fragment structure records internally linked textual groups; their complete order is not securely authorial.

contentParts.label: Fragment I (Group A)
contentParts.summary: General Prologue; Knight, Miller, Reeve, and Cook sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: Fragment II (Group B1)
contentParts.summary: Man of Law introduction, prologue, tale, and epilogue. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: Fragment III (Group D)
contentParts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: Fragment IV (Group E)
contentParts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: Fragment V (Group F)
contentParts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: Fragment VI (Group C)
contentParts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: Fragment VII (Group B2)
contentParts.summary: Shipman, Prioress, Chaucer's Sir Thopas and Melibee, Monk, and Nun's Priest sequence; verse and prose. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: Fragment VIII (Group G)
contentParts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: Fragment IX (Group H)
contentParts.summary: Manciple prologue and tale. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: Fragment X (Group I)
contentParts.summary: Parson prologue, prose tale, and prose Retraction. This is one of the ten internally linked fragments; its position in a complete sequence is editorially chosen because Chaucer left no final order.

contentParts.label: The General Prologue
contentParts.summary: Robinson's I (A) unit titled “The General Prologue”; the XML represents it with 860 L elements. The L count is an encoding metric aligned to Robinson's lineation, not a new critical line count.

contentParts.summary: Robinson's I (A) unit titled “The Knight's Tale”; the XML represents it with 2,253 L elements, of which three unnumbered Latin Explicit rubrics are not verse lines. dateStatements.display: Begun probably in the late 1380s and worked on through the 1390s; unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame; exact dates are conjectural. The XML header's inherited 1387–1394 range is narrower than current scholarly caution.
The production range overlaps Chaucer's lifetime and the years immediately after his death; it is a witness date, not a composition date.

Calling Hengwrt the first known attestation relies on a qualified scholarly judgment that it is generally accepted as the earliest assembly attempt. Its [1395]–[1405] range overlaps Ellesmere's c. 1400–1410 range, so the catalog dates alone do not prove strict priority.

extent.description: The work has twenty-four conventional pilgrim tales. CT contains ten DIV1 fragment groups and fifty-four titled DIV2 units, with 23,448 non-note L elements, 33 LG elements, and 249 PB milestones. Of those L elements, 17,414 occur in verse components and 6,034 serialize the prose Tale of Melibee, Parson's Tale, and Retraction. Ten unnumbered Latin Explicit rubrics are serialized as L inside four verse components; excluding those rubric elements yields the semantic verse-line total of 17,404 recorded here. These are encoding-derived metrics, not a new critical lineation.

formStatements.description: Predominantly verse, with substantial prose in the Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale and a prose Retraction. The XML serializes prose in L elements, which does not make those components verse.

scope.description: Especially the General Prologue's social portraits
A constituent mode, serious and parodic; not the genre of every tale
Constituent fabliaux; the Shipman's Tale and Merchant's Tale also have fabliau affinities
The Second Nun's Tale as a saint's life of Cecilia
The Prioress's Tale as a miracle of the Virgin

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Robinson's edited Middle English and its digital encoding should not be conflated with the exact spelling of either Ellesmere or Hengwrt.
Robinson's editor credit is displayed because CT is textually derived from his edition; it does not make the 1957 edition the cataloging subject.
The XML's ten DIV1 labels say Group 1–10. Fragment roman numerals and Chaucer Society letter groups are scholarly crosswalks used for human readability.
Prose encoded in L elements is cataloged as prose; encoding shape is not textual form.

next_steps: Preserve the broad late-1380s-to-1400 range for the composite work.
Consult tale-specific textual scholarship before assigning dates to constituent tales.
openQuestions.question: Which individual tales predate the pilgrimage frame, and how late did Chaucer continue revising the collection?

next_steps: Treat Robinson's sequence as an editorial arrangement rather than an authorial fact.
Expose fragment labels and ordering uncertainty in the viewer.
openQuestions.question: What order, if any, did Chaucer intend for the ten fragments?

next_steps: Retain the complete nested part inventory in CT.
Create independent records only when component-level research can avoid inheriting whole-collection claims uncritically.
openQuestions.question: Should the twenty-four tales eventually receive independent work records with their own sources, dates, genres, and adaptation relationships?

notes: London is a literary and dialect association, not a securely documented composition site for the whole work.

scope.description: Robinson's 1957 source edition represented by CT, not authorship of the medieval work

subjects.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: A company of pilgrims traveling from Southwark toward the shrine of Thomas Becket at Canterbury agree to tell stories. Chaucer uses the frame to juxtapose social portraits, competing voices, literary genres, moral arguments, and performances ranging from courtly romance, a saint's life, and a Marian miracle to fabliau, beast fable, sermon, and prose instruction. The work survives unfinished and without a securely authorial final ordering of all ten fragments.

CT represents ten fragments and fifty-four titled DIV2 units: the General Prologue; twenty-four tales; tale-specific prologues, introductions, interruptions, links, and epilogues; and the Retraction. The encoded order follows Robinson's 1957 edition and its Ellesmere-related arrangement.

This record catalogs Chaucer's composite work as represented by CT. Robinson's pp. 17–265, the U-M/OTA encoding, two repository XML versions, and the Ellesmere and Hengwrt manuscript objects are transmission layers described in the linked lineage manifest rather than parts of the work itself.

notes: Capitalization follows the U-M item and XML header; this is the digital item's title form, not evidence of an authorial medieval title.
A manuscript heading in Hengwrt, not treated here as a uniquely authorial title for the abstract work.
titles.value: Here bygynneth the Book of the tales of Cauntbury

work.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
work.dateDisplay: Begun in the late 1380s; worked on into the 1390s, unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
work.regionDisplay: England; associated with late-medieval London literary language; exact place of composition uncertain

subjects.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

work.summary: A company of pilgrims traveling from Southwark toward the shrine of Thomas Becket at Canterbury agree to tell stories. Chaucer uses the frame to juxtapose social portraits, competing voices, literary genres, moral arguments, and performances ranging from courtly romance, a saint's life, and a Marian miracle to fabliau, beast fable, sermon, and prose instruction. The work survives unfinished and without a securely authorial final ordering of all ten fragments.
